parent.label: Transcripts
parent.description: Written records of spoken language — the closest training data gets to the oral register that humans default to in conversation. YouTube auto-captions, professional subtitles, podcast transcripts. This data teaches models how people actually talk: contractions, incomplete sentences, disfluencies, colloquialisms.
bullets: YouTube Data API: auto-generated captions for hundreds of millions of videos
OpenSubtitles: 3.7M subtitle files across 60+ languages, sourced from film and TV
Spoken language is structurally different from written — simpler syntax, more deixis ('this,' 'here,' 'now')

cells.label: YouTube Captions
cells.description: Auto-generated and manually-corrected captions from YouTube videos — lectures, tutorials, commentary, news, cooking shows, interviews. The auto-caption system has reached the point where the transcription errors themselves appear in training data.
bullets: YouTube hosts 800M+ videos; auto-captions are available for most English content
3Blue1Brown, Khan Academy, MIT OpenCourseWare: lecture content with visual explanations transcribed
YouTube transcripts collapse time — a 20-minute video becomes a 2,000-word document
cells.screenshotCaption: YouTube — auto-captions from hundreds of millions of videos feed into transcript datasets

cells.label: Subtitles
cells.description: OpenSubtitles and similar movie/TV subtitle databases — the dialogue of cinema. This is conversational language at its most compressed and intentional: characters speaking in ways that carry story, reveal character, and advance plot.
bullets: OpenSubtitles: 3.7M files across 60+ languages, covering decades of film and television
Screenplay dialogue teaches the relationship between context and appropriate response
Available in dozens of languages with some alignment across language versions of the same film
cells.screenshotCaption: OpenSubtitles — 3.7M subtitle files from film and television worldwide
